title: Where did "I have to keep moving" come from?
subtitle: A journalling set for tracing the origin of the pattern that makes stopping feel dangerous.
excerpt: A journal prompt set for exploring the learned pattern of needing to stay in motion: where it came from, who modelled it, what it is protecting against, and what rest would look like if it felt truly safe. Five prompts, twenty minutes.
sourceNotes: Drawn from Day 5 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Feel safe in stillness."

practice.whenToUse: Use when you notice a pattern of needing to be busy and want to examine where it came from.

content.text: A journal set for tracing the keep-moving pattern back to where it learned that stopping wasn't safe. Five prompts. Allow twenty minutes.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
What does stopping feel like in your body?
Not what you think about stopping, what it physically feels like to sit down with nothing to do.
Who in your life modelled constant movement? Who was always busy, always doing?
Name the person. Write what you saw.
What does the pattern believe will happen if you stop?
Write the fear underneath the movement. Behind laziness? Behind falling apart? Behind something being missed?
Has the pattern ever actually protected you from the thing it fears? Or does it mostly keep you tired?
What would you let yourself do in the evenings if stopping felt safe?
Drawn from Day 5 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive to Fall Asleep Faster and Wake Energised (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Feel safe in stillness."
